title: Entanglement entropy across the dynamical phase transition in the quantum $\mathcal{O}(N)$ model
authors: Frederick del Pozo, Tangi Morvan, Irénée Frérot, Nicolas Cherroret
abstract: We demonstrate that the dynamical phase transition of the quantum $\mathcal{O}(N)$ model at large $N$ leaves universal fingerprints in the infrared structure of the entanglement spectrum. While the leading contribution to the entanglement entropy at long time follows the conventional volume law associated with ballistic entanglement spreading, its subleading behavior sharply distinguishes the different dynamical regimes. Specifically, quenches at and below the critical point generate gapless low-energy entanglement modes together with logarithmic corrections to the long-time entanglement entropy, whose scaling is governed by the dynamical exponent of the transition. Using an infinite-slab bipartition geometry and exact numerical correlation functions in the large-$N$ limit, we characterize these scaling laws across the dynamical phase diagram and relate them to the emergence of long-range correlations during the post-quench dynamics. We further show that the entanglement eigenmodes themselves reveal characteristic signatures of the dynamical phase transition through their spatio-temporal structure and degeneracy properties.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the dynamical phase transition in the quantum O(N) model leaves unique signatures in the subleading terms of the entanglement entropy and the structure of the entanglement spectrum. It shows that while the leading term follows a volume law, the subextensive logarithmic corrections and the scaling of entanglement modes change significantly at the critical point. This provides a way to identify dynamical phase transitions using entanglement-based observables.
main_problem: Investigating how a dynamical phase transition (DPT) in the quantum O(N) model manifests in the entanglement entropy and entanglement spectrum.
main_result: The DPT is revealed through subleading logarithmic corrections to the volume-law entanglement entropy and characteristic changes in the entanglement dispersion scaling (e.g., from q^1/2 to q^1/4).
method: Analytical and numerical study in the large-N limit using a self-consistent Gaussian theory, the Williamson approach for entanglement spectrum, and an infinite-slab geometry.
model_or_system: The quantum O(N) model in the large-N limit, specifically a system of O(N) bosons with quartic interactions in three spatial dimensions following a sudden mass quench.
key_observables: Entanglement entropy (EE), entanglement spectrum (ES), entanglement gap, and two-point correlation functions.
important_parameters: Quench depth (delta), dynamical exponent (alpha), subsystem size (L), and time (t).
main_assumptions: The study relies on the large-N limit, which makes the dynamics integrable and solvable via Gaussian theory.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the quench phase diagram; Figure 3 illustrates light-cone spreading of EE; Figure 4 shows the entanglement gap scaling; Figure 6 shows data collapse for the inverse gap; Figure 9 displays the spatial structure of entanglement eigenvectors.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the model and quench protocol, establishes the numerical framework for entanglement calculation, analyzes the scaling of the entanglement spectrum and gap across the DPT, and concludes with the spatial structure of entanglement modes.
why_interesting: It provides universal fingerprints of non-equilibrium phase transitions through entanglement signatures, which is highly relevant for understanding many-body dynamics and the emergence of long-range correlations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Effective mass reff(t) in dimension d = 3, obtained by numerically solving Eqs. (3) and (4) for δ = 0.001 (purple curve), δ = 0 (red curve) and δ = −9 (green curve), corre- sponding to quenches above, at, and below the critical point, respectively. Here we choose ri = 100 and u = 10, yield- ing a critical value rc ≃−0.43. The numerical simulations are performed for k ∈[0, 10Λ] and 105 points. The dashed line indicates the asymptotic behavior reff(t) = act−2, where ac = d

FIG. 2. Panel (a) Spatial geometry considered in this work. We compute the entanglement entropy S(A|B) in the O(N) model by considering a parallelepiped subsystem A of vol- ume LL2 ∥with transverse extent L∥→∞. The system is numerically discretized, with a grid step a equal in each spa- tial direction and fixed by the maximum radial momentum kmax in panel (b). Panel (b) Corresponding discretization of momenta in the O(N) model. kz is discretized in Ntot steps within [−π/a, π/a], with Ntot setting the full system size Ltot = Ntota.

FIG. 3. Main panel: Entanglement entropy per unit area as a function of subsystem size at increasing times after a quench into the ordered phase (δ = −1), showing the light- cone spreading of entanglement. Deep inside the light cone, L ≪2ct, the entropy becomes time independent and ap- proaches S(L, t) ∝L (red dotted line), displaying the volume- law scaling characteristic of entanglement spreading following the quench. Outside the light cone, L > 2ct, where correla- tions have not yet propagated across the subsystem, the en- tropy instead obeys an area law, S(L, t) ∝L0, inherited from the initial ground state, while increasing linearly in time. Pa- rameters: u = 10, ri = 100, and N∥= Ntot...

FIG. 4. Panel a: Late-time entanglement spectrum ωj q∥as a function of q∥for three different quench depths: above (δ = 0.001), at (δ = 0) and below (δ = −4) the critical point of the DPT. The different dashed curves correspond to different entanglement modes j, with the dispersion of the lowest-lying mode j = 0 highlighted by the darker curve. Spectra are shown at finite time t = 200. The dispersion of the lowest- lying zero-mode at zero momentum, ∆(t) ≡ωj=0 q∥=0, defines the entanglement gap. Panel b: Momentum dispersion of the lowest-lying mode (j = 0) at low momentum in a log–log scale, for different quench depths δ and at a fixed long time t = 103 (while δ = 0 is shown for t = 104)....

FIG. 5. Prefactor of the entanglement dispersion, ω0 q∥/q1/2 ∥ with q∥→0, as a function of the quench depth |δ| into the or- dered phase (red dots). The values of this ratio are extracted from fits of the dispersion curves in Fig. 4b, performed at the time t = 103. The solid red curve corresponds to the algebraic fit 0.026|δ|−0.498. Parameters: ri = 100, u = 10, Λ = π/2, Ntot = 5 × 105, and Ns = 100.
